name: At-Tawwab

names.en: The Ever-Relenting, the Acceptor of Repentance

title: At-Tawwab: The Ever-Relenting, the Acceptor of Repentance
meta_description: At-Tawwab, the One who keeps turning back to His servants, built from the Qur'an and classical tafsir. The two turnings of repentance, Adam's first words, and the three who were boycotted fifty nights until the earth closed in. Qur'an verified.
keywords: At-Tawwab, At-Tawwaab, The Acceptor of Repentance, The Ever-Relenting, names of Allah, 99 names, tawba, repentance in Islam, how Allah accepts repentance, Surah At-Tawbah, Ka'b ibn Malik, Adam repentance, Asma ul Husna, Buruja
source_note: Grounded in the Qur'an (Sahih International, verified via quran.ai) and classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, Tafsir as-Sa'di, and al-Tafsir al-Muyassar), in the voice of Buruja.
lede: There is a particular kind of distance a person can feel from God, and it is not the distance of someone who never believed. It is the distance of someone who did believe, and then slipped, and now cannot quite face the One they slipped in front of. You know the prayer you have been putting off. You know the habit you keep meaning to leave. And underneath it sits a quiet suspicion that you have used up your turns, that the door you walked away from has been closed behind you. This name was revealed to break that suspicion.

At-Tawwab, the One who keeps turning back. Not a God who reluctantly forgives once and warns you not to come again, but One whose very name, in the intensive Arabic form, means He turns to the returning servant again and again, however many times they come. And the deeper you read into it, the more astonishing it becomes: the scholars find in this name two turnings, not one. He turns the servant back to Himself first, and then He turns to the servant in acceptance. Your repentance was His mercy before it was your choice.

sections.h2: The name, and the form it is built on

ayah.arabic: أَلَمْ يَعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِ وَيَأْخُذُ الصَّدَقَاتِ وَأَنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: Do they not know that it is Allāh who accepts repentance from His servants and receives charities and that it is Allāh who is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful?

paras: Start with the word itself. At-Tawwab comes from three Arabic letters, ta, waw, ba, the root of tawba. And the root means, at its heart, to turn, to return, to come back. When a servant makes tawba, the picture in the word is of someone turning around, away from where they were walking and back toward the One they walked away from. So tawba is not first about feeling bad. It is about turning back.

Now notice the shape the name is pressed into. It is not the plain form of the verb. At-Tawwab sits on an Arabic pattern the language keeps for something intense, abundant, done again and again, a quality so settled it defines the one who carries it. The same pattern gives you words for a person who is constantly, habitually something rather than occasionally. So At-Tawwab does not mean the One who once accepted a repentance. It means the One whose turning back toward His servants never stops, who keeps relenting, keeps receiving, keeps reopening the door, as many times as it is knocked on.

Hear how al-Sa'di puts it, commenting on this very verse. At-Tawwab, he writes, means abundant in turning back toward those who repent: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated from them many times over. Allah does not grow weary of turning to His servants, al-Sa'di adds, until they themselves grow weary and refuse anything but to flee and bolt from His door. Read that again slowly. The limit on His relenting is never reached from His side. It is only ever reached from ours.

sections.h2: The thing the verse is doing to you
paras: It matters where this name lands in the Qur'an. In Surah At-Tawbah it arrives right after Allah mentions accepting repentance from His servants and receiving their charity, both in the same breath. Ibn Kathir reads the verse as a tahyij, a stirring up, an active encouragement toward the two things that wipe a sin away: turning back, and giving. Each of them, he says, erases sins and scrubs them out.

And he carries a striking image from the Prophet ﷺ to show how seriously Allah takes what you bring back to Him. Allah accepts the charity and takes it in His right hand, the hadith says, and nurtures it for one of you the way one of you raises his foal, until the single morsel becomes like the mountain of Uhud. Ibn Kathir even relays the saying of Ibn Mas'ud that the charity falls into the hand of Allah before it falls into the hand of the beggar. Hold that beside the name. The same God who grows a date into a mountain is the God who receives the small, embarrassed turning of a servant who thought it was too little and too late.

So the verse is not informing you of a divine attribute from a distance. It is reaching for your sleeve. Do you not know, it asks, what He is? He is the One who accepts. Why, then, are you still standing outside?

sections.h2: The two turnings hidden in the name

ayah.arabic: فَتَلَقَّىٰ آدَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِ كَلِمَاتٍ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

paras: Here is the part of this name most people never hear, and it changes everything. Go back to the very first repentance in human history, Adam after the slip in the garden. The verse says he received words from his Lord, and then He turned to him. Adam did not invent the words of his own repentance. He received them. They were handed to him.

Al-Sa'di stops on exactly this and draws out the structure. His turning, he says of Allah, is of two kinds: first His granting the servant the success to repent, and then, second, His acceptance of the repentance once its conditions are met. And of His mercy toward them, al-Sa'di continues, is that He granted them the success to repent, and pardoned them, and overlooked. So the movement runs like this. Before you ever turned back to Allah, Allah had already turned toward you, softening your heart, placing the regret in your chest, putting the words on your tongue. Your repentance reaching up is the visible half. The invisible half is His mercy reaching down first to make it possible.

Ibn Kathir fills in what those received words were. He carries the report that they are the famous plea preserved elsewhere in the Qur'an, our Lord, we have wronged ourselves, and if You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers. And he relays from Mujahid a fuller form of Adam's supplication, sealed with this very name: O Allah, there is no god but You, glory and praise be to You, my Lord, I have wronged myself, so turn to me, indeed You are At-Tawwab, the Merciful. Even the words Adam used to ask were a gift from the One he was asking.

_Note: the two-turnings framing here is exactly how al-Sa'di glosses 2:37 (tawfiq first, then qabul), presented as his reading and offered for contemplation, not as a separate scholarly category beyond the tafsir._

sections.h2: He turned to them so that they would turn

ayah.arabic: وَعَلَى الثَّلَاثَةِ الَّذِينَ خُلِّفُوا حَتَّىٰ إِذَا ضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ الْأَرْضُ بِمَا رَحُبَتْ وَضَاقَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ وَظَنُّوا أَن لَّا مَلْجَأَ مِنَ اللَّهِ إِلَّا إِلَيْهِ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ لِيَتُوبُوا ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: And [He also forgave] the three who were left alone [i.e., boycotted, regretting their error] to the point that the earth closed in on them in spite of its vastness and their souls confined [i.e., anguished] them and they were certain that there is no refuge from Allāh except in Him. Then He turned to them so they could repent. Indeed, Allāh is the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

paras: If you want to feel the two turnings in a single life, sit with the three who were left behind. Ibn Kathir preserves the whole story in the long, agreed-upon report of Ka'b ibn Malik. When the Prophet ﷺ set out for the expedition to Tabuk, Ka'b stayed behind, not out of hypocrisy and not for any real excuse, but through pure procrastination. He kept meaning to prepare and kept telling himself there was time, until the army was gone. As he himself says in the report, he had never been stronger or more able to go than in the very campaign he failed to join.

When the Prophet ﷺ returned, the hypocrites came to him with oaths and excuses, and he accepted their outward words and left their hearts to Allah, more than eighty of them. Then Ka'b came. And here is the hinge of the whole story. He could have offered a smooth excuse too. Instead he said, by Allah, I have no excuse, I was never more free or more able than when I stayed behind. The Prophet ﷺ said, as for this one, he has told the truth, so stand until Allah decides about you.

What followed was the closing in the verse describes. The Prophet ﷺ forbade the Muslims to speak to the three of them, and for fifty nights Ka'b lived inside a silence. He would give salam and watch to see if the Prophet's ﷺ lips even moved to return it. The streets he knew went strange to him. He climbed the wall of his own cousin, Abu Qatada, the most beloved of people to him, and gave him salam, and the man would not answer. A letter even arrived from the king of Ghassan offering him refuge and honour somewhere else, and Ka'b recognised it for what it was, another test, and burned it in the oven. The earth, for all its width, had become too narrow to hold him.

Read how al-Sa'di explains the turn when it finally comes. Then He turned to them, he says, means He permitted their repentance and granted them the success to make it, so that, the verse continues, they would repent, that it would actually occur from them, and then Allah would accept it from them. There it is again, unmistakable. He turned to them so that they would turn. The relief reached Ka'b at dawn on the fiftieth night: a man called out from the top of a mountain at the top of his voice, O Ka'b ibn Malik, rejoice. Ka'b fell down in prostration, knowing relief had come. And when he reached the Prophet ﷺ, his face was shining like a piece of the moon, and he said, rejoice with the best day that has passed over you since your mother bore you.

Do not miss what al-Sa'di lifts out of this. The sign that good is coming and the hardship is lifting, he writes, is that the heart attaches itself completely to Allah and cuts itself off from depending on creation. The very phrase the Qur'an uses is that they were certain there was no refuge from Allah except to Him. There is no fleeing away from Him. There is only fleeing toward Him. The narrowness was not Allah abandoning them. It was Allah herding them home.

sections.h2: What real turning back actually is

ayah.arabic: إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا وَأَصْلَحُوا وَبَيَّنُوا فَأُولَٰئِكَ أَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنَا التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: Except for those who repent and correct themselves and make evident [what they concealed]. Those - I will accept their repentance, and I am the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

paras: Because His door never closes, it is easy to imagine tawba is a light thing, a word said quickly and then forgotten. The mufassirun are gentle about Allah's side of it and exact about ours. Commenting on this verse, al-Sa'di spells out what those who repent means: that they return from the sins they were committing, with regret, and a leaving off, and a firm resolve not to go back. And then more: it is not enough to abandon the ugly deed, he says, until you also do the good one, and in the case of one who concealed truth it is not enough until he makes clear what he hid. Turning back is regret, plus stopping, plus the inward decision not to return, plus, where you can, repair.

And then, having set the bar honestly, al-Sa'di lands on the mercy of the name. Whoever brings the cause of tawba, he writes, Allah turns to him, because He is At-Tawwab, the One who keeps returning to His servants with pardon and overlooking after the sin when they repent, and with kindness and blessings after withholding when they come back. Notice the second half of that. The name does not only mean He forgives the past. It means He resumes the giving. The relationship does not limp back wounded. He turns to you with ihsan, with new good, as though you had not left.

There is a tenderness in how al-Sa'di reads it that is easy to walk past. Of His mercy, he says, is that He granted them the success to repent so they repented and returned, then He had mercy on them by accepting it from them, out of gentleness and generosity. Both ends of your repentance, the strength to make it and the acceptance of it once made, are described here as His mercy, not your achievement. You contribute the turning. Even that, He made possible.

sections.h2: The name even the prophets reached for

ayah.arabic: رَبَّنَا وَاجْعَلْنَا مُسْلِمَيْنِ لَكَ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِنَا أُمَّةً مُّسْلِمَةً لَّكَ وَأَرِنَا مَنَاسِكَنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَا ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
ayah.text: Our Lord, and make us Muslims [in submission] to You and from our descendants a Muslim nation [in submission] to You. And show us our rites [of worship] and accept our repentance. Indeed, You are the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

paras: Here is something that should reorder how you think about who needs this name. Picture Ibrahim and his son Ismail, raising the foundations of the Ka'ba, two of the greatest human beings who ever lived, in the middle of an act of pure obedience. And listen to how they seal their prayer: and turn to us, indeed You are At-Tawwab, the Merciful.

Al-Sa'di explains why these two, of all people, ask this in this moment. Since the servant, whoever he is, he writes, must inevitably fall into shortcoming and stands in need of repentance, they said, and turn to us, indeed You are At-Tawwab, the Merciful. Sit with that. Even at the height of devotion, building the very House people would face in prayer for the rest of time, the prophets did not assume they were above needing to be turned back to. They built their tawba into their worship.

This quietly dismantles a lie the heart loves to tell, that you must first become clean, and then approach. The pattern of the prophets is the reverse. You approach in order to be made clean. Tawba is not the toll you pay before the relationship resumes. It is woven into the relationship the whole way through. The most beloved servants of Allah were also the most constant in turning back to Him, and they wrote that turning straight into their du'a.

sections.h2: Even the way you treat people falls under it

ayah.arabic: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اجْتَنِبُوا كَثِيرًا مِّنَ الظَّنِّ إِنَّ بَعْضَ الظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ ۖ وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا وَلَا يَغْتَب بَّعْضُكُم بَعْضًا ۚ أَيُحِبُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن يَأْكُلَ لَحْمَ أَخِيهِ مَيْتًا فَكَرِهْتُمُوهُ ۚ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ رَّحِيمٌ
ayah.text: O you who have believed, avoid much [negative] assumption. Indeed, some assumption is sin. And do not spy or backbite each other. Would one of you like to eat the flesh of his brother when dead? You would detest it. And fear Allāh; indeed, Allāh is Accepting of Repentance and Merciful.

paras: We tend to file repentance under the big, private sins, the ones we are ashamed of in the dark. But look where the Qur'an plants this name in Surah Al-Hujurat. It comes at the close of a verse about the everyday sins of the tongue and the heart toward other people: suspicion, spying, backbiting. Allah even gives the unforgettable image, would any of you love to eat the flesh of his dead brother, and then seals it, and fear Allah, indeed Allah is At-Tawwab, the Merciful.

Al-Sa'di reads the placement of the name as both warning and door. The verse, he says, is a severe warning against backbiting, and a sign that backbiting is among the major sins, because Allah likened it to eating the flesh of the dead. And yet the verse does not end on the threat. It ends on the name. He defines it right here: At-Tawwab is the One who permits His servant's repentance and grants him the success for it, then turns to him by accepting it, merciful to His servants in that He called them to what benefits them. So even the sin you commit against your brother, behind his back, with your words, is met at the end of the verse by the open door of the One who keeps turning back.

Al-Muyassar renders the close of the verse simply: indeed Allah is ever-relenting toward His believing servants, merciful to them. There is a mercy in where this name sits. It tells you that the slips of the tongue you barely notice are real, serious enough that Allah names them in His Book. And in the same line it tells you the way back from them is the same as the way back from anything: turn, and you will find Him already turning to you.

sections.h2: Live as someone who can always come back
paras: A name of Allah is never only something to know. It is meant to remake the way you live, and At-Tawwab remakes you in at least three ways.

First, it kills despair at the root. The single most dangerous lie after a sin is that it is too late, that you are too far gone, that you have asked too many times already. Al-Sa'di answered it directly: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated many times over, and Allah does not weary of accepting repentance until His servants weary of asking. The door is not a one-time door. So the right response to falling is not to lie where you fell, deciding the relationship is over. It is to get up and turn around again, today, knowing the One you are turning to has been waiting, facing you the whole time.

Second, it changes the speed of your return. Look at what nearly destroyed Ka'b ibn Malik and what saved him. What endangered him was delay, putting off the thing he meant to do until the moment passed. What saved him was the truth, told plainly and at once: by Allah, I have no excuse. The pattern for the believer is to make tawba honest and immediate, to refuse the smooth excuse we tell even ourselves, and to come back without the long detour through self-justification.

Third, it makes you gentle with other people who are trying to come back. The God whose name is At-Tawwab keeps reopening His door to the same servants again and again. A heart that has truly tasted that cannot stand at its own little door turning people away, cannot greet someone else's repentance with suspicion, cannot remind a person forever of the sin they have already left. You were received when you thought you were finished. Now you become someone who makes it easy for others to come back, the way it was made easy for you.

Step back and let the whole shape of it settle. The fear underneath so much of our distance from God is that the door has closed, that we have spent our turns, that the One we let down is now turned away from us. At-Tawwab is the Qur'an's answer to that fear, and it is not a soft reassurance. It is a name of God, built on a form that means again, and again, and again.

And it answers the fear more completely than you dared hope. You were afraid you had to find your own way back. But the scholars show you the way back was His mercy before it was your effort: He turned the three home by closing the earth around them until there was nowhere left to flee but to Him, He handed Adam the very words of his repentance, He turned to them so that they would turn. Your regret, your longing to return, the fact that you are even reading this and feeling the pull, is itself the first half of His turning toward you.

So whatever it is you have been carrying, the prayer you stopped praying, the thing you keep going back to, the distance you assumed was permanent, hear the name and turn around. He is At-Tawwab. He was facing you before you turned. The door you thought was shut was never a door He closed. It was only a back you needed to turn around.

dua.arabic: رَبَّنَا وَتُبْ عَلَيْنَا ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ التَّوَّابُ الرَّحِيمُ
dua.translit: Rabbana wa tub alayna innaka anta at-Tawwab ar-Rahim
dua.en: Our Lord, and accept our repentance. Indeed, You are the Accepting of Repentance, the Merciful.

lessons.bold: Never decide the door is closed.
lessons.text: Al-Sa'di teaches that whoever turns to Allah, He turns to them, even if the sin is repeated many times, and that He never wearies of accepting repentance until the servant wearies of asking. After a fall, do not lie where you fell. Get up and turn around again.

lessons.bold: Let your repentance be His mercy, not your achievement.
lessons.text: On 2:37 al-Sa'di describes two turnings: Allah first grants you the success to repent, then accepts it. The regret in your chest and the words on your tongue are already gifts. Stop waiting to feel worthy, and turn.

lessons.bold: Come back quickly, and come back honest.
lessons.text: Procrastination nearly ruined Ka'b ibn Malik; the plain truth, by Allah I have no excuse, is what saved him. Refuse the smooth excuse you tell even yourself. Make your tawba immediate and truthful.

lessons.bold: Build turning back into your worship.
lessons.text: Even Ibrahim and Ismail, raising the Ka'ba, sealed their prayer with You are At-Tawwab. You do not become clean and then approach. You approach in order to be made clean. Weave istighfar through your good days, not only your worst ones.

lessons.bold: Reopen your own door to others.
lessons.text: The One who keeps receiving you back again and again makes you someone who receives others. Do not meet a person's repentance with suspicion or hold their old sin over them forever. You were received when you thought you were finished.

faq.q: What does the name At-Tawwab mean?
faq.a: At-Tawwab (التواب) means the Acceptor of Repentance, or the Ever-Relenting, from the root t-w-b, which means to turn or return. It is built on an intensive Arabic form, so it does not mean the One who accepted a repentance once, but the One who keeps turning back to His servants again and again. Commenting on Surah At-Tawbah 9:104, al-Sa'di explains it as abundant in turning toward those who repent: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated many times, and Allah does not weary of accepting repentance until the servant himself wearies of asking.

faq.q: What are the two turnings of repentance?
faq.a: Commenting on Adam's repentance in Al-Baqarah 2:37, al-Sa'di notes that Allah's turning is of two kinds: first He grants the servant the success (tawfiq) to repent, and then He accepts the repentance once its conditions are met. In other words, before you turn back to Allah, Allah has already turned toward you, softening the heart and even, in Adam's case, handing him the very words of his repentance. Your reaching up is the visible half; His mercy reaching down to make it possible is the first half. (This framing is al-Sa'di's reading of the verse, offered for reflection.)

faq.q: Is there a limit to how many times Allah will accept my repentance?
faq.a: According to al-Sa'di's commentary on 9:104, the limit is never reached from Allah's side. He writes that whoever turns to Allah, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated many times over, and that Allah does not grow weary of turning back to His servants until they grow weary and refuse anything but to flee from His door. So the door does not close after a set number of returns; the only way it closes is if the servant stops coming. (See also 2:37, 2:128, 2:160, and 49:12, where the name recurs.)

On Al-Baqarah 2:160, al-Sa'di lists what 'those who repent' means: returning from the sin with regret, leaving it off, and a firm resolve not to return to it. He adds that it is not enough to abandon the bad deed without doing the good one, and that someone who concealed a truth must also make clear what they hid. So true tawba is regret, stopping, the inward decision not to go back, and, where possible, repair. And al-Sa'di stresses that the strength to do all of this is itself part of Allah's mercy, not the servant's achievement alone.

faq.q: How is At-Tawwab different from Al-Ghaffar, the Ever-Forgiving?
faq.a: The two names are close companions and the Qur'an often pairs the meaning of repentance with forgiveness. The emphasis differs. Al-Ghaffar centres on the forgiveness itself, the covering and wiping away of the sin. At-Tawwab centres on the turning: the movement of Allah back toward the servant, and the servant back toward Allah. As al-Sa'di puts it on 2:160, At-Tawwab keeps returning to His servants with pardon after the sin and with renewed kindness and blessing after withholding, so the name speaks not only to a sin being erased but to the whole relationship being turned back and resumed.

closing_heading: Why this name stays with us
closing: We carry a quiet fear that we have used up our turns, that the door we walked away from has been shut behind us. At-Tawwab is the answer the Qur'an gives to that fear, not as a comforting feeling but as a name of God, built on a form that means again, and again, and again. He is the One who handed Adam the words of his repentance, who closed the wide earth around three boycotted men for fifty nights until there was nowhere left to flee but to Him, who turned to them so that they would turn, and whom even Ibrahim and Ismail called upon while building His House. The scholars find two turnings in this name: He turns the servant home first, then He turns to the servant in acceptance. Your very longing to come back is already the first half of His mercy. To know this name is to never again believe that it is too late.

O Allah, At-Tawwab, the Ever-Relenting, You turn to Your servants before they turn to You, and You keep turning to them however many times they come. Turn our hearts back to You, grant us the strength to return, and accept it from us when we do. Make our repentance honest and quick, keep us from despairing of Your door, and let us be people who hold our own door open to others the way You held Yours open to us. Rabbana wa tub alayna innaka anta at-Tawwab ar-Rahim.

slides:
At-Tawwab.

The One who keeps turning back.

There is a distance from God that belongs to the person who believed, then slipped, and now cannot face Him. Who suspects they have used up their turns.

This name was revealed to break that suspicion.

The name comes from the root t-w-b: to turn, to return.

And it sits on an intensive Arabic form. So At-Tawwab does not mean the One who forgave once.

It means the One who keeps turning back to His servants. Again. And again. And again.

Al-Sa'di on this name:

Whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, **even if the disobedience were repeated from them many times over.**

Allah does not weary of accepting repentance until the servant himself wearies of asking.

Here is the part most people never hear.

The scholars find **two turnings** in this name.

Al-Sa'di on Adam (2:37): first Allah grants the success to repent, then He accepts it.

He turns the servant home first. Then He turns to him.

Adam did not invent the words of his repentance.

The Qur'an says he **received** them from his Lord.

Even the words you use to ask are a gift from the One you are asking.

Feel it in one life: the three boycotted after Tabuk.

Ka'b ibn Malik stayed behind, no excuse, just delay. For fifty nights no one spoke to him. The earth, the Qur'an says, **closed in on him for all its width.**

Until a voice called from a mountain: O Ka'b, rejoice.

Read the exact words Allah uses:

'Then He turned to them **so that they could turn.**' (9:118)

Al-Sa'di: He permitted and enabled their repentance, so that it would happen, and then He would accept it.

He turned to them so that they would turn.

Even Ibrahim and Ismail, building the Ka'ba, sealed their prayer:

'...and turn to us. Indeed You are **At-Tawwab**, the Merciful.' (2:128)

You do not become clean and then approach. You approach in order to be made clean. 🤍

So whatever you have been carrying, the prayer you stopped, the thing you keep returning to, the distance you assumed was permanent:

He is At-Tawwab. He was facing you before you turned.

The door you thought was shut was only a back you needed to turn around.
social.caption:
There is a quiet fear that lives under a lot of our distance from God: that we have used up our turns, that the door is closed.

At-Tawwab, the Acceptor of Repentance, the Ever-Relenting, is the answer the Qur'an gives. Grounded in classical tafsir (Ibn Kathir, al-Sa'di, al-Muyassar), the name comes from the root t-w-b, to turn back, in an intensive form: the One who keeps turning to His servants again and again. Al-Sa'di says it plainly: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the sin is repeated many times, and Allah never wearies of accepting repentance until the servant wearies of asking.

The most astonishing part is what the scholars call the two turnings. On Adam's repentance (2:37), al-Sa'di explains that Allah first grants the servant the success to repent, and then accepts it. Adam did not invent his words of repentance; he received them from his Lord. Your very longing to come back is already the first half of His mercy.

You see it in the three boycotted after Tabuk: Allah closed the wide earth around them until there was nowhere to flee but to Him, then, in the Qur'an's exact words, 'He turned to them so they could turn' (9:118). And even Ibrahim and Ismail, raising the Ka'ba, sealed their prayer with 'You are At-Tawwab.'

So turn around. He was facing you before you moved. 🤍

Save this, and read the full reflection: buruja.com/names-of-allah/at-tawwab

#AtTawwab #NamesOfAllah #AsmaUlHusna #99Names #Tawba #Repentance #IslamicReminders #Quran #Buruja #Islam

email.subject: The door you thought was closed
email.alt_subject: The name built on again, and again, and again
email.preview: At-Tawwab, the One who keeps turning back. The two turnings of repentance, and why your longing to return is already His mercy.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

There is a particular kind of distance from God, and it does not belong to the person who never believed. It belongs to the one who did believe, then slipped, and now cannot quite face the One they slipped in front of. You know the prayer you have been putting off. You know the habit you keep meaning to leave. And underneath sits a quiet suspicion that you have used up your turns, that the door has closed behind you.

One of the names of Allah was revealed to break that suspicion: At-Tawwab, the Acceptor of Repentance, the Ever-Relenting.

The name comes from the root t-w-b, which means to turn, to return. When a servant repents, the picture in the word is of someone turning around, back toward the One they walked away from. And the name sits on an intensive Arabic form, so it does not mean the One who accepted a repentance once. Commenting on Surah At-Tawbah 9:104, the scholar al-Sa'di explains it as the One who keeps turning back to those who repent: whoever turns to Him, He turns to them, even if the disobedience were repeated many times, and Allah does not weary of accepting repentance until the servant himself wearies of asking. The limit is never reached from His side.

Now here is the part most of us never hear. The scholars find two turnings in this name, not one. Commenting on Adam's repentance (Al-Baqarah 2:37), al-Sa'di writes that Allah's turning is of two kinds: first He grants the servant the success to repent, and then He accepts the repentance once it is made. Look at the verse: Adam received words from his Lord. He did not invent the words of his own repentance. He received them. So before you ever turn back to Allah, Allah has already turned toward you, placing the regret in your chest and the words on your tongue. Your reaching up is the visible half. His mercy reaching down to make it possible is the first half.

You can feel both turnings in one life. Ibn Kathir preserves the long, agreed-upon report of Ka'b ibn Malik, one of the three left behind after the expedition to Tabuk. Ka'b had no excuse; he simply procrastinated until the army was gone. For fifty nights the Muslims were told not to speak to him. He would greet the Prophet, peace and blessings be upon him, and watch to see if his lips even moved to answer. He climbed the wall of his own beloved cousin, who would not return his salam. The earth, in the Qur'an's words, closed in on him for all its width. And then relief came at dawn: a man called from the top of a mountain, O Ka'b, rejoice. Read the exact words Allah uses for it: 'Then He turned to them so they could repent' (9:118). Al-Sa'di explains: He permitted and enabled their repentance, so that it would happen, and then He would accept it. He turned to them so that they would turn.

And if you think this name is only for the great private sins, look where the Qur'an also plants it: at the end of a verse about suspicion and backbiting (Al-Hujurat 49:12). Even the sins of the tongue against your brother are met by the open door of the One who keeps turning back. And even Ibrahim and Ismail, building the Ka'ba in the middle of pure obedience, sealed their prayer with 'and turn to us, indeed You are At-Tawwab, the Merciful' (2:128). The prophets did not become clean and then approach. They approached in order to be made clean.

So whatever you have been carrying, hear the name and turn around. He is At-Tawwab. He was facing you before you turned. The door you thought was shut was never a door He closed.

Ya Tawwab, turn our hearts back to You, and accept it from us when we do.

If you would like the full reflection, we have written it here:
